enrichment.summary: Kyle records a late-night Thanksgiving 2016 vlog describing his day: dinner at his mom's, seeing the film Arrival (which confused him until his brother and a YouTube explainer clarified the time-loop plot), and returning home to continue an obsessive ancient Rome mapping project. He reflects on how the Rome work keeps pulling him deeper and thinks he could spend a solid month on it before burning out.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Good movie, confusing until explained — the alien time-loop concept is cool and possibly reflects how life actually works
Increasingly captivating; could fall into it for a whole month before burning out, and would have something real to show for it
Thorough relative to other cities he's worked on but only about 10% of what it could be

stances.topic: Mike Duncan's History of Rome content
stances.position: Positive — recommends checking it out, was listening until his computer stopped cooperating

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I got high before working this morning.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I worked until about 3:30 AM.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Today I got up, worked, went to my mom's house for Thanksgiving meal, and then watched the movie Arrival.

claims.subject: Arrival
claims.claim: I was confused about the movie Arrival because they kept showing her daughter and then revealed Jeremy Renner was the father.

claims.subject: Arrival
claims.claim: My brother told me that the events with the daughter hadn't happened yet in the timeline.

claims.subject: Arrival
claims.claim: I watched a YouTube explanation of Arrival and now it makes sense.

claims.subject: Arrival
claims.claim: The movie is about aliens who live with no time, creating a continuous loop where they fix things by going back in time.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I think life might actually be like that, with no linear time.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: After the movie, I went back to my mom's house briefly and then came home to work.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I've been working for about four days, but I'm not entirely sure because I lose track of time.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I never pay attention to how long I've been working on a project.

claims.subject: Google Earth
claims.claim: The most interesting thing about Google Earth's ancient Rome from 205 AD is that it keeps getting more fascinating to me.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: When I start a new project, I ask myself if it's worth my time, especially if it's something I've never done before.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm willing to dedicate a month to a new project if it's compelling enough.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: If I work on a project for a month or more, I'll have a solid foundation to return to later.

claims.subject: Rome
claims.claim: Rome is the one city that fascinates me across two different time periods.

claims.subject: Rome
claims.claim: The current map of Rome only shows about 10% of what it could be.

claims.subject: Rome
claims.claim: Rome is thoroughly documented, with all its history available, which is pretty cool.

claims.subject: Mike Duncan
claims.claim: I've been listening to Mike Duncan's podcast about Rome, but my computer won't play it anymore.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: My computer has been acting up, preventing me from watching YouTube videos or accessing comments.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: My German friend Dave's comments were marked as spam, and approving them just deleted them.

claims.subject: Vitruvius
claims.claim: I'm currently listening to Vitruvius, specifically his discussions about aqueducts.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm studying aqueducts in detail to gather all the necessary data for my project.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I should probably type in Italian to find more accurate sources on Roman aqueducts.
